drills.scenario: Maria runs a Shopify pet treats brand. Her best ad set is converting at a $22 CPA on a 3-million-person audience and has racked up 14 purchases in the last 7 days. She's excited and wants to grow it fast, so she's thinking about jumping straight to a brand-new campaign at 10x the budget.
drills.question: According to the Five-Tier Scaling System, what should she do FIRST?
options: Jump to Large Budget Scaling (Tier 4) since the offer is hot
Start with Duplication Scaling (Tier 1) — copy the winning ad set at the same starting budget and same Post ID
Use Small Budget Scaling (Tier 5) and raise budget 20% every 3 days
Combine all five tiers at once for Massive Rapid Scaling
drills.explanation: The Five-Tier System is ordered: try method 1 before 2, 2 before 3, etc. Her ad set meets the Tier 1 readiness bar (>=10 conversions, CPA below target and under the $100 ceiling, audience >1M), so Duplication Scaling is the correct starting point. Skipping straight to aggressive budget scaling is exactly the pitfall the system warns against.

drills.scenario: A local gym is advertising a 7-day trial. One ad set has only 4 sign-ups so far this week at a $31 CPA (target is $40), running on a niche local audience of about 180,000 people. The owner wants to start duplicating it tonight.
drills.question: What's the best diagnosis of this situation?
options: Go ahead and duplicate — the CPA is below target so it's ready
Hold off — the ad set hasn't hit the 10-conversion confidence bar yet, and the audience is well under 1M
Switch immediately to Large Budget Scaling because trials convert fast
Duplicate but use a brand-new Post ID for each copy
drills.explanation: Pre-scale readiness requires at least 10 conversions in the ad set with CPA below target before you scale. With only 4 conversions there isn't enough data to be confident. The ~180K audience is also far below the ideal >1M for Duplication, which caps ad-set count and drives frequency up fast. Wait for more data.

drills.scenario: Devon coaches a webinar funnel and has a winning ad set he wants to clone via Duplication Scaling in Ads Manager.
drills.question: Put the Duplication Scaling steps in the correct order.
options: Set the calendar to the last seven days
Sort ad sets by CPA, smallest to largest
Screen by conversion volume and select all ads at or below your CPA
Click Create Similar Ad Set, then review and Place Order
Rename the auto-created '-copy' ad sets to 'DUP' plus the date or version number
Hit Edit, go to Budget and Schedule, and enter the daily budget
Select the duplicates, click Enter Post ID, insert the original ad's Post ID, and Submit
drills.explanation: Duplication Scaling follows a fixed sequence: set the 7-day window, sort by CPA smallest-to-largest, screen by conversion volume and select winners, Create Similar Ad Set and Place Order, rename the '-copy' sets to DUP+date for tracking, set the budget, then apply the ORIGINAL ad's Post ID. Working from data (sort then screen) before creating, and consolidating social proof via the same Post ID, are the points of the order.

drills.scenario: An e-commerce skincare brand has a hot ad set converting at a $25 target CPA. The media buyer wants to use Large Budget Scaling (Tier 4) by duplicating the winner into a NEW campaign.
drills.question: What daily budget reflects Tier 4 best practice?
options: $10/day — the same as the original ad set
$50-$75/day — about 2-3x the target CPA
$250/day — roughly 10x target CPA to move fast
$500/day — the more aggressive the better
drills.explanation: Large Budget Scaling best practice is to set the daily budget to 2-3x your target CPA. At a $25 CPA that's $50-$75/day. Being more aggressive than 3x CPA only works about 1 in 5 times and is reserved for a really hot offer; the original $10/day isn't 'large budget' scaling at all.

drills.scenario: A bootstrapped meal-prep startup has a tight budget and just one strong ad set at $10/day hitting a $14 target CPA. They want the most conservative, low-risk way to grow spend without spooking the algorithm.
drills.question: Using Small Budget Scaling (Tier 5), what's the right move?
options: Raise the budget 15-20% every 3 days while CPA stays below target; if CPA exceeds target, roll back at least two increments
Double the budget every day and judge results after 24 hours
Raise the budget 50% daily and never roll back
Jump straight to 3x CPA on day one
drills.explanation: Small Budget Scaling raises the budget ~15-20% every three days as long as CPA stays below target, then rolls back at least two increments once target CPA is exceeded. The 3-day cadence respects Facebook's ~72-hour adjustment window, and gradual increments avoid the 'jerky rollercoaster' caused by sudden large jumps.

drills.scenario: A SaaS founder bumped a winning ad set from $100/day to $300/day. Two days later they're alarmed: CPA spiked, results look chaotic, and Facebook seems to have throttled delivery. They ask what went wrong and whether to keep cutting and re-raising the budget.
drills.question: What's the best diagnosis and fix?
options: The creative is fatigued — swap in entirely new images immediately
Sudden 3x budget jump triggered the algorithm's 'punish' behavior; also they're reading results inside the 72-hour adjustment window — let it settle, and prefer gradual or duplication-based scaling
Facebook hates SaaS offers — switch to a different objective
The audience is too large — narrow it to under 100k
drills.explanation: Naive budget multiplication causes the 'jerky rollercoaster': Facebook can punish a campaign whose 'order' (budget) suddenly changes, and results are skewed for ~72 hours while the algorithm settles. Judging at 2 days is premature. The fix is structured scaling (e.g., duplicate into a new campaign or step up gradually) and allowing the adjustment window before reading results.

drills.scenario: An online language school wants to apply Tier 4 Large Budget Scaling carefully and avoid the common mistakes that wreck the algorithm.
drills.question: Which of these are correct Tier 4 best practices and cautions? (Select all that apply)
options: Duplicate the lowest-CPA ad sets into a NEW campaign
Set the daily budget to 2-3x your target CPA
Launch the new campaign in the morning, not the evening
When increasing budget a lot, change the lower end of the age range by one year so Facebook treats it as a different audience
Routinely push past 3x CPA because aggression always pays off
drills.explanation: Tier 4 moves winners into new campaigns at 2-3x target CPA, launched in the mornings (evenings skew pacing), and the 'ninja trick' of nudging the lower age bound by one year stops new sets from competing with existing ones. The false option is pushing past 3x CPA routinely — that only works ~1 in 5 times and is reserved for a hot offer.

drills.scenario: A travel-gear Shopify brand wants to broaden reach. Their Michigan Method campaign uses 20 interest targets and is converting well. They want to grow into NEW audiences without disturbing the winner.
drills.question: What does Audience Scaling (Tier 2) call for here?
options: Keep the same 20 interests but triple the budget
Duplicate the entire Michigan Method campaign, then replace the original 20 interests with 20 NEW interests, keeping the same creative
Replace the creative with 9 brand-new ad variations on the same interests
Roll back the budget by two increments and wait 3 days
drills.explanation: Audience Scaling broadens reach by duplicating the entire Michigan Method campaign and swapping the original 20 interests for 20 new interests while keeping the same creative. Changing the creative on the same audience is Alteration Scaling (Tier 3); changing budget alone is not audience expansion.
